title: Why Cats Make Weird Open-Mouth Face (Flehmen) 🐱
description:
The verified science behind the flehmen response and Jacobson's organ in cats. Research on the vomeronasal system explains the distinctive open-mouth facial expression revealing advanced chemical communication.

Sources: Patrick Pageat Pherosynthese Institute, Journal of Comparative Physiology A 2019, Sharon Crowell-Davis University of Georgia, Veterinary Behavior research 2020, Karen McComb University of Sussex.

#FlehmenResponse #JacobsonsOrgan #CatBehavior #CatScience

segments.voice: Your cat just made that strange open-mouth grimace face, called the flehmen response. This isn't your cat being weird. The expression activates a specialized sensory organ called Jacobson's organ that lets cats literally taste smells. The science behind this dual sensing system is more sophisticated than most owners realize.
segments.visual: cat flehmen response open mouth grimace face detail

segments.voice: The Jacobson's organ, formally called the vomeronasal organ, sits in the roof of the cat's mouth behind the upper incisors. Two small openings called incisive papillae connect this organ to the nasal cavity. Patrick Pageat documented the structure in 2003 research that established its function in feline chemical communication.
segments.visual: cat jacobsons organ anatomy mouth roof location diagram

segments.voice: The flehmen response opens the mouth and curls back the upper lip to expose the incisive papillae. This positioning allows pheromones and chemical signals trapped on the tongue to access the Jacobson's organ. The behavior takes 3 to 8 seconds and processes complex chemical information.
segments.visual: flehmen mechanism cat mouth opening tongue pheromone access

segments.voice: The Jacobson's organ contains receptor cells distinct from normal olfactory cells. Standard smell detection uses nasal receptors connected to the olfactory bulb. Vomeronasal receptors connect to the accessory olfactory bulb, processing information in entirely separate brain regions. Cats experience two distinct chemical sensing channels simultaneously.
segments.visual: olfactory versus vomeronasal receptor brain pathway diagram

segments.voice: Pheromones cats specifically detect through this system include F1 sexual pheromones, F3 facial marking pheromones, F4 allomarking pheromones, and F5 maternal appeasement pheromones. Each pheromone category triggers specific behavioral responses that owners observe but cannot smell themselves due to human anatomical differences.
segments.visual: cat pheromone types f1 f3 f4 f5 detection chart

segments.voice: Male cats display flehmen responses more frequently than females. The behavior peaks during exposure to female cat urine, particularly during estrus cycles. Sharon Crowell-Davis at University of Georgia documented 4-times higher flehmen frequency in intact male cats compared to neutered males or female cats in 2015 research.
segments.visual: male cat flehmen response detecting female pheromones

segments.voice: The behavior is not limited to romantic contexts. Cats perform flehmen responses when investigating other cats' urine markings, examining unfamiliar objects, processing strong odors, and analyzing food. The vomeronasal system provides additional chemical information beyond what normal smell processing reveals.
segments.visual: cat flehmen response investigating various stimuli urine objects

segments.voice: Horses, snakes, and many mammals share the flehmen response. The behavior is most pronounced in cats because of their highly developed vomeronasal system. Humans possess a vestigial Jacobson's organ but lack functional receptor cells. We cannot experience the chemical sensing that drives feline flehmen responses.
segments.visual: different animals flehmen response comparison cats horses

segments.voice: The duration of flehmen response correlates with information complexity. Brief 2-second responses indicate simple chemical identification. Extended 8-second responses suggest detailed analysis of multiple compounds. The cat is essentially performing chemical reading at the molecular level during this distinctive facial expression.
segments.visual: flehmen response duration variation simple complex analysis

segments.voice: Kittens develop flehmen response capability around 6 weeks of age. Before this developmental milestone, the Jacobson's organ exists but the neural connections to brain regions are not fully formed. Adult cats use the system throughout life, with the behavior remaining functional well into senior years.
segments.visual: kitten development flehmen response 6 weeks formation

segments.voice: Spraying behavior in cats serves a chemical communication purpose detected through Jacobson's organ in other cats. The urine contains felinine, a sulfur-containing amino acid that breaks down into volatile compounds. These compounds carry detailed information about the marking cat's identity, status, and reproductive state.
segments.visual: cat spraying behavior chemical communication felinine

segments.voice: Facial marking through cheek and chin rubbing deposits F3 pheromones on surfaces. Other cats detect these markings through both normal smell and Jacobson's organ analysis. The dual sensing provides complete information about who marked the territory, when, and their current emotional and physiological state.
segments.visual: cat facial marking pheromone deposit detection

segments.voice: Cat litter design affects pheromone communication and Jacobson's organ stimulation. Strongly scented litters can overwhelm the vomeronasal system, causing cats to avoid their litter boxes. Unscented or lightly scented options preserve chemical communication channels while maintaining odor control for human owners.
segments.visual: cat litter scented unscented pheromone communication impact

segments.voice: Feliway and similar synthetic pheromone products work by replicating natural cat pheromones detected through Jacobson's organ. Veterinary research has confirmed these products produce measurable behavioral changes in cats. The products effectively communicate with cats through their existing chemical detection systems.
segments.visual: synthetic pheromone products feliway calming cat stress

segments.voice: Karen McComb at University of Sussex documented in 2019 research that the flehmen response can be triggered by human chemical signatures. When humans return home after extended absence or after physical exertion, cats may perform flehmen analysis on their owners. The cats are gathering chemical information about human activities and emotional states.
segments.visual: cat performing flehmen response on human owner returning home

segments.voice: The vomeronasal system processing occurs in the accessory olfactory bulb, then transmits to the amygdala and hypothalamus. These brain regions handle emotional responses and hormonal regulation. Chemical signals detected through Jacobson's organ directly influence cat behavior at a deeper level than visual or auditory information processing.
segments.visual: cat brain vomeronasal processing accessory olfactory bulb

segments.voice: Bobcats and other wild felines display the flehmen response identically to domestic cats. The behavior is conserved across all 38 cat species, indicating its fundamental evolutionary importance. Wild cats use vomeronasal sensing for territory establishment, mate identification, and predator detection across all known felid habitats.
segments.visual: bobcat lynx wild felines flehmen response identical

segments.voice: The Jacobson's organ in large cats like tigers and lions is proportionally larger relative to other anatomical structures. Big cats display the flehmen response with dramatic head positioning and clearly visible mouth opening. The behavior is highly visible in zoo and wildlife observation contexts.
segments.visual: tiger lion flehmen response dramatic mouth opening

segments.voice: Veterinary behaviorists use flehmen response frequency as a diagnostic indicator. Reduced flehmen response in previously normal cats can suggest neurological issues, vomeronasal organ damage, or systemic disease affecting sensory function. Detailed history-taking about this behavior helps veterinarians identify potential underlying medical conditions.
segments.visual: veterinary behaviorist examining cat flehmen frequency diagnostic

segments.voice: Certain plant compounds trigger flehmen responses reliably. Catnip, silver vine, valerian root, and tatarian honeysuckle all contain compounds that interact with the vomeronasal system. The chemical similarities to cat pheromones explain why these plants produce behavioral responses including flehmen analysis.
segments.visual: catnip silver vine triggering cat flehmen response

segments.voice: Olfactory and vomeronasal systems operate independently but coordinate behaviorally. Cats may show normal olfactory function while having impaired vomeronasal function, or vice versa. The two systems provide complementary information that combine to create complete chemical perception of the environment around the cat.
segments.visual: olfactory vomeronasal systems independent coordinated function

segments.voice: Spraying versus flehmen response are different but related behaviors. Spraying deposits pheromones for other cats to detect. Flehmen response analyzes deposited pheromones from other cats. Together these behaviors create a sophisticated chemical communication network operating constantly between cats within shared territories.
segments.visual: spraying versus flehmen cat communication network

segments.voice: Research into the vomeronasal system continues to reveal new functions. Recent studies suggest the system may detect environmental toxins, identify food spoilage, and even sense atmospheric pressure changes. The full scope of vomeronasal capabilities in cats remains an active area of veterinary science research.
segments.visual: ongoing vomeronasal research expanding known cat capabilities

segments.voice: Multi-sensory integration in cats combines vomeronasal information with vision, hearing, and standard smell to create complete environmental awareness. The flehmen response represents one component of this complex sensory processing. Cats experience their world through multiple simultaneous channels human consciousness cannot fully replicate.
segments.visual: cat multi sensory integration vomeronasal vision hearing

segments.voice: Most owners never realize the weird face their cat makes represents one of the most sophisticated chemical detection systems in the animal kingdom. The flehmen response is not strange behavior but advanced sensory analysis revealing information about pheromones, identities, and environmental chemistry humans simply cannot perceive.
segments.visual: cat flehmen response sophisticated sensory analysis revealed

segments.voice: Tomorrow's video covers cat sleep cycles and REM dreams, including the actual research on what cats dream about and how their sleep patterns differ from human sleep. Subscribe to discover the science behind your cat's 16-hour daily sleep schedule.
segments.visual: cat sleeping rem dream cycle research
